danger_signals.visual_form: Enemy sprites in overworld and dungeon navigation
danger_signals.meaning: Transition to side-view battle perspective signals combat danger

danger_signals.visual_form: Side-on perspective shift with large enemy sprite
danger_signals.meaning: Perspective change from top-down to side-view indicates elevated threat level and gameplay mode transition

danger_signals.visual_form: Interior dungeon tileset with enemy placement
danger_signals.meaning: Enclosed spaces with scattered enemies communicate containment and resource scarcity

danger_signals.visual_form: King Erik's castle collapse visual in narrative
danger_signals.meaning: Environmental destruction communicates invasion success and political collapse

safety_signals.visual_form: Detailed town graphics with NPC-populated streets
safety_signals.meaning: Presence of NPCs and structured settlements signal sanctuary and resource availability

safety_signals.visual_form: Interior shop screens accessed via NPC interaction
safety_signals.meaning: Merchant presence and transaction opportunities communicate stability and civilization
Exploration perspective without immediate combat indicates safe traversal state

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: King Erik's castle (burned ruin); small village of Wyclif (refuge location)

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Enemy sprites representing Tsarkon's invading army
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Invading force presence throughout realm; scattered rings of good placed as deterrents

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: King Erik's castle intact with full court; peaceful kingdom infrastructure
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Kings Erik and Tsarkon cooperate; realm in balance

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Castle collapse visual; defensive battles; enemy army presence in overworld
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Defenders overwhelmed; royal family scattered; hero (Blade) forced to flee with prince

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Corrupted realm with enemy territories; scattered rings throughout landscape; towns under siege or transformed
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Prince begins quest at age 18; must collect rings of good; Tsarkon's forces actively hunt the prince
Tsarkon's curse prevents family reunion; motivates protagonist quest completion

environmental_storytelling.example: Town graphics show detailed NPC populations and shops vs. corrupted/defended settlements

environmental_storytelling.technique: Ring placement as world corruption markers
environmental_storytelling.example: Rings of good scattered by Tsarkon throughout realm; collecting them reverses corruption and restores balance
Burned castle of King Erik visible in world; physical testament to Cartahena's military victory
Prince's mother transformed to living stone; remains in realm as magical corruption indicator and emotional story anchor
Wyclif village established as refuge sanctuary; visual design differs from war-torn locations

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Lives peacefully in Wyclif village under Blade's guardianship
Travels overworld; enters dungeons; collects rings; engages in real-time sword and magic combat
Invades Excalabria; scatters rings; hunts prince; maintains occupation forces throughout realm
Appear in overworld and dungeons; trigger side-view perspective battles when encountered
Operate shops; provide equipment and magic; offer information through dialogue

notes: Sword of Vermilion uses perspective shifts as its primary world-state communication system: top-down for exploration/safety, side-view for danger/combat. The game's narrative core—rings of good vs. rings of evil—is visually manifested through item collection mechanics. Faction identity is communicated through environmental possession (Cartahena occupies the realm; Excalabria is fragmented) rather than color coding or sigils. The corrupted mother serves as both environmental storytelling and emotional motivation. Town graphics intentionally detailed versus rough 3D battle screens create visual hierarchy of safe vs. dangerous spaces.
